excerpt: Rising Treasury yields are drawing capital away from Bitcoin, increasing crash risk as investors seek safer bonds. Analysts link this trend to hawkish Federal Reserve policies. Bitcoin's price remains vulnerable as bond yields continue to rise globally.

content.introduction: Rising Treasury yields are drawing capital away from Bitcoin, increasing crash risk as investors seek safer bonds. Analysts link this trend to hawkish Federal Reserve policies. Bitcoin's price remains vulnerable as bond yields continue to rise globally.
body: # Bitcoin (BTC) price analysis: Crash risk rises as bond yields surge. price analysis: Crash risk rises as bond yields surge"). That is how Holger Zschaeptiz, one of the most widely followed macro commentators on X, reacted after the yield on the 30-year U.S. Treasury note (government bond) rose to 5% early today, hitting the highest since July 2025. His reaction also sums up the mood of several crypto analysts who see rising yields as a headwind for bitcoin BTC$75,735.40, the world's biggest cryptocurrency by market value and a macro asset. As long as yields remain attractive and [Fed's monetary policy] stays tight, capital has a real alternative to risk. When the U.S. government needs to borrow money, it issues bonds, and the yield on those bonds is the annual return the bond investors earn. Note that the 30-year yield is not the only one rising. Bond yields are also rising in the U.K. and other parts of the world.
Bitcoin price bounced 9%, but leverage and a 90% profit surge now warn of a possible crash toward the $58000 support level. Read more here!
# Ouch. The U.S. 30-year Treasury yield just hit 5% and bitcoin may pay the price

Rising bond yields increase pressure on Bitcoin, as they make bonds more attractive, leading to capital rotation out of risk assets. Bitcoin's price fell 2% to $75,670. Higher yields reflect tighter monetary policy and inflation concerns.

Here's why rising bond yields typically hurt BTC and other risk assets. When the U.S. government needs to borrow money, it issues bonds, and the yield on those bonds is the annual return the bond investors earn. So, when yields rise, bonds become more attractive. A 30-year Treasury yielding 5% is an almost risk-free return.

Therefore, every dollar sitting in bitcoin is a dollar not earning that 5% yield. That tradeoff typically leads to capital rotation out of non-yielding risk assets, such as bitcoin and other risky assets like technology stocks. Rising yields also typically weigh on gold, which fell over 1% to a one-month low of $4,540 on Wednesday and last changed hands near $4,564. [...] "At this point, the dynamic is simple. As long as yields remain attractive and [Fed's monetary policy] stays tight, capital has a real alternative to risk. This continues to pressure assets like crypto, depending on liquidity and momentum," Diana Pires, chief business officer at sFOX, said in an email to CoinDesk. sFOX is a San Francisco-based cryptocurrency prime dealer and trading platform designed for institutional investors, hedge funds, and businesses.

Bitcoin is already under pressure alongside an uptick in the Dollar Index (DXY). As of writing, BTC traded at $75,670, down 2% over 24 hours, and the DXY hovered above 99, looking to extend Wednesday's 0.5% gain. [...] ## Oil rally is lifting inflation expectations

The bond yield surge is not just about the Fed. Early Thursday, oil prices surged to their highest since 2022, with Brent briefly topping $125 per barrel, after Trump mulled extending the blockade of Iranian ports. Moreover, oil prices have been elevated, hovering largely between $80 to $120 since the Iran war began in late February.

As a result, energy prices at gas stations are surging, pushing long-term inflation expectations higher, as CoinDesk noted early this week.

All of that is pushing yields higher.

Bitcoin is already under pressure alongside an uptick in the Dollar Index (DXY). As of writing, BTC traded at $75,670, down 2% over 24 hours, and the DXY hovered above 99, looking to extend Wednesday's 0.5% gain.

Here's why rising bond yields typically hurt BTC and other risk assets. When the U.S. government needs to borrow money, it issues bonds, and the yield on those bonds is the annual return the bond investors earn. So, when yields rise, bonds become more attractive. A 30-year Treasury yielding 5% is an almost risk-free return. [...] His reaction also sums up the mood of several crypto analysts who see rising yields as a headwind for bitcoin BTC$75,735.40, the world's biggest cryptocurrency by market value and a macro asset.

"At this point, the dynamic is simple. As long as yields remain attractive and [Fed's monetary policy] stays tight, capital has a real alternative to risk. This continues to pressure assets like crypto, depending on liquidity and momentum," Diana Pires, chief business officer at sFOX, said in an email to CoinDesk. sFOX is a San Francisco-based cryptocurrency prime dealer and trading platform designed for institutional investors, hedge funds, and businesses. [...] Therefore, every dollar sitting in bitcoin is a dollar not earning that 5% yield. That tradeoff typically leads to capital rotation out of non-yielding risk assets, such as bitcoin and other risky assets like technology stocks. Rising yields also typically weigh on gold, which fell over 1% to a one-month low of $4,540 on Wednesday and last changed hands near $4,564.

"Rising Treasury yields and a stronger dollar [have] historically pressured crypto valuations by tightening financial conditions," Vikram Subburaj, CEO of India-based FIU-registered Giottus exchange, said.

Moreover, as central banks worldwide moved away from ultra-loose monetary policy, Japan was under pressure to follow suit. The result was a synchronized global yield rise, in which Japan could no longer remain an outlier without risking capital flight or excessive currency depreciation.

## How Do Rising Bond Yields Affect Bitcoin’s Price

Rising bond yields—especially those of U.S. Treasuries—have a significant influence on Bitcoin’s price, although the relationship is nuanced and context-dependent. [...] Ultimately, rising yields are a double-edged sword for Bitcoin. When driven by healthy economic growth and tight monetary policy, they tend to hurt Bitcoin by raising the opportunity cost of holding it. But when yields rise due to inflation fears, debt sustainability concerns, or loss of confidence in traditional assets, Bitcoin can benefit as a non-sovereign alternative. As such, the direction of Bitcoin’s price in a high-yield environment depends on the broader narrative driving those yields. [...] Another major driver is hawkish central bank policy. The Federal Reserve, having aggressively raised interest rates from near zero to around 5% since 2022, signaled a "higher-for-longer" stance throughout much of 2024. Even as it started cutting rates later that year, markets remained skeptical of a swift dovish pivot, and yields on the 10- and 30-year Treasuries continued to climb.

which means arbitrageurs short Bitcoin and long Ethereum simultaneously. This would surely drive down Bitcoin price and contribute to its crash risk. Another reason is that sophisticated investors are reluctant to chase a target after a big price jump, for the upcoming higher volatility (Driessen and Maenhout, 2013) may cause a greater risk. When conservative investors witnessed a price surge in Bitcoin, they would turn to buy its close neighbor Ethereum to control downside risk, which means buying power on Bitcoin is diminishing. And things become even worse after Ethereum catches up with Bitcoin, because neither of them is attractive any more to sophisticated investors. Consequently, the crash risk of Bitcoin surges. [...] crisis sentiment has a noticeable positive impact on cryptocurrencies’ market price crash risk (Anastasiou et al., 2021).

The surge in Treasury yields has broader implications for financial markets. Higher yields increase borrowing costs across the economy, from mortgages to corporate loans, and can tighten financial conditions. This environment typically weighs on risk assets by increasing financing costs and reducing the relative appeal of equities and cryptocurrencies. Bitcoin, for instance, has shown resilience in recent weeks, trading within a narrow range between $68,000 and $71,000 despite the macroeconomic headwinds. [...] The U.S. 30-year Treasury yield has climbed to 5%, its highest level in two decades, signaling a shift in investor sentiment and potentially increasing pressure on risk assets such as Bitcoin and technology stocks. The rise in bond yields reflects heightened inflation expectations and a broader reevaluation of risk-return tradeoffs in the current economic environment. As yields climb, bonds become more attractive relative to non-yielding or lower-yielding assets, prompting investors to reallocate capital toward fixed-income instruments. [...] logo

# US Treasury Yield Hits 5%: What It Means for Bitcoin and Risk Assets

The US 30-year Treasury yield has risen to 5%, its highest level in two decades, potentially pressuring bitcoin and other risk assets. Rising bond yields make bonds more attractive, leading investors to rotate capital out of non-yielding assets like bitcoin and technology stocks. The 10-year yield is also elevated, pointing to financial tightening and disincentivizing risk-taking.
